frontText: Move the cursor left, down, up and right without arrow keys
backText:
```
h  j  k  l
```

`h` left, `j` down, `k` up, `l` right. Keeping your fingers on the home row is the foundation of Vim speed.

[Left-right motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#left-right-motions)

frontText: Jump to the start of the **next** / **previous** word
backText:
```
w    next word start
b    previous word start
e    end of current/next word
```

Prefix with a count: `3w` jumps three words forward. Capital `W`/`B`/`E` treat punctuation as part of the word (WORD motions).

[Word motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#word-motions)

frontText: Jump to the **start** of the line, the **first non-blank** character, and the **end** of the line
backText:
```
0    column 0
^    first non-blank character
$    end of line
```

`^` is usually what you want when editing indented code.

[Left-right motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#left-right-motions)

frontText: Jump to the **first** / **last** line of the file, and to line **42**
backText:
```
gg     first line
G      last line
42G    line 42   (or :42)
```

Any count before `G` jumps to that absolute line number.

[Up-down motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#up-down-motions)

frontText: Jump to the **previous** / **next** empty line (paragraph boundary)
backText:
```
{    previous empty line
}    next empty line
```

Great for hopping between functions or code blocks separated by blank lines.

[Object motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#object-motions)

frontText: Scroll **half a page** down / up, and a **full page** down / up
backText:
```
Ctrl-d   half page down
Ctrl-u   half page up
Ctrl-f   full page down
Ctrl-b   full page up
```

Half-page scrolling (`Ctrl-d`/`Ctrl-u`) keeps more visual context than full-page jumps.

[Scrolling](https://vimhelp.org/scroll.txt.html)

frontText: Move the cursor to the **top**, **middle** and **bottom** of the visible screen, and **center** the current line
backText:
```
H    top of screen (High)
M    middle of screen
L    bottom of screen (Low)
zz   center current line on screen
```

`zt` and `zb` scroll the current line to the top / bottom instead.

[Various motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#various-motions)

frontText: Jump to the next occurrence of the character `x` **within the current line**, then repeat the jump
backText:
```
fx   jump onto next 'x'
Fx   jump onto previous 'x'
tx   jump just before next 'x'
;    repeat last f/F/t/T
,    repeat in opposite direction
```

Combines with operators: `dfx` deletes up to and including the next `x`.

[Left-right motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#left-right-motions)

frontText: Jump **back** to where you were before the last jump, then **forward** again
backText:
```
Ctrl-o   older position in jump list
Ctrl-i   newer position in jump list
`.       jump to position of last edit
```

Searches, `G`, `gg`, `%` etc. all record entries in the jump list, so `Ctrl-o` is the universal "take me back".

[Jump motions](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#jump-motions)

frontText: Delete (cut) the current line / **3** lines / from cursor to **end of line**
backText:
```
dd    delete current line
3dd   delete 3 lines
D     delete from cursor to end of line
```

Deleted text goes into the unnamed register, so a following `p` pastes it — `dd` + `p` moves a line.

[Deleting text](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#deleting)

frontText: Insert a new line **below** / **above** the current line and start typing
backText:
```
o    open line below, enter Insert mode
O    open line above, enter Insert mode
```

Both respect auto-indentation of the surrounding code.

[Inserting text](https://vimhelp.org/insert.txt.html)

frontText: Copy (yank) the current line and paste it **below** / **above**
backText:
```
yy   yank current line   (3yy yanks 3 lines)
p    paste below current line
P    paste above current line
```

`yyp` duplicates the current line.

[Copying and moving text](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#copy-move)

frontText: Select several lines, **copy** them, and insert them **after another line**
backText:
```
V        start line-wise Visual mode
j / k    extend the selection
y        yank the selection
(move to target line)
p        paste the copied lines below it
```

Use `d` instead of `y` to **move** the lines instead of copying.

[Visual mode](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html)

frontText: Copy lines **5–9** after line **20**, and move lines **5–9** after line **20** — without Visual mode
backText:
```vim
:5,9t20    copy (transfer) lines 5-9 after line 20
:5,9m20    move lines 5-9 after line 20
```

`:t` and `:m` take any range; `.` means current line, `$` last line — e.g. `:.,+3t$` copies the current 4 lines to the end of the file.

[:copy and :move](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#copy-move)

frontText: Join the line below onto the end of the current line
backText:
```
J     join with one space between
gJ    join without inserting a space
```

A count works too: `3J` joins three lines into one.

[Simple changes](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#simple-change)

frontText: Change **every occurrence** of the word under the cursor, one by one, confirming each (native "multi-cursor")
backText:
```
*      search for word under cursor
cgn    change the next match
(type replacement) Esc
.      apply the same change to the next match
n      skip a match without changing it
```

`gn` selects the next search match, so `cgn` + `.` replays the change match-by-match — Vim's plugin-free answer to multiple cursors.

[gn](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#gn)

frontText: Search for the **word under the cursor** forward / backward
backText:
```
*    search forward for word under cursor
#    search backward for word under cursor
```

Matches whole words only (`\<word\>`). Use `g*` / `g#` for partial-word matches. `n` / `N` step through the results.

[Search commands](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#search-commands)

frontText: Start a **column (block) selection** and extend it
backText:
```
Ctrl-v         start Visual Block mode
h j k l        extend the block
fx  /  $       extend to a char / to line ends
```

Visual Block selects a rectangle of text instead of whole lines — the basis for all column editing.

[Visual Block mode](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#visual-block)

frontText: Insert the same text at the **beginning of many lines at once** (e.g. comment out a block with `// `)
backText:
```
Ctrl-v     start block selection in column 0
j j j      extend down over the lines
I// Esc    insert '// ' at the left edge of every line
```

The inserted text appears on all selected lines the moment you press `Esc`. Use `A` instead of `I` to append at the right edge of the block.

[Blockwise insert](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#v_b_I)

frontText: Append text at the **end of multiple lines** even when the lines have different lengths
backText:
```
Ctrl-v     start block selection
j j j      extend down
$          extend the block to each line's own end
A;  Esc    append ';' at the end of every line
```

The `$` makes the block "ragged right", so `A` appends at each line's actual end instead of a fixed column.

[Blockwise append](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#v_b_A)

frontText: **Change** or **delete** a selected column of text on every line
backText:
```
Ctrl-v + motions   select the block
c  (new text) Esc  replace block on every line
d  or  x           delete the column
rx                 replace every selected char with 'x'
```

With `c`, you type the replacement once and it is applied to all lines on `Esc`.

[Visual operators](https://vimhelp.org/visual.txt.html#visual-operators)

frontText: Turn a column of identical numbers (e.g. `0`) into an **increasing sequence** (1, 2, 3, …)
backText:
```
Ctrl-v     select the column of numbers
g Ctrl-a   increment as a sequence
```

Plain `Ctrl-a` in Visual mode adds 1 to every number; `g Ctrl-a` adds 1 to the first, 2 to the second, 3 to the third… — instant numbered lists.

[v_g_CTRL-A](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#v_g_CTRL-A)

frontText: Indent / un-indent the **current line**, and **3 lines**
backText:
```
>>    indent current line
<<    un-indent current line
3>>   indent 3 lines
```

The shift amount is controlled by the `shiftwidth` option.

[Shifting lines](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#shift-left-right)

frontText: Indent a **visual selection**, then repeat the indent on the same lines
backText:
```
V + j/k    select lines
>          indent  ( <  un-indents )
gv         reselect the same lines
>          indent again   (or just press . )
```

Visual selections collapse after one shift — `gv` or `.` lets you shift further.

[Shifting lines](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#shift-left-right)

frontText: **Auto-indent** the current line, a selection, and the **whole file**
backText:
```
==      auto-indent current line
=       auto-indent Visual selection
gg=G    re-indent the entire file
```

`=` applies the indent rules of the current filetype — the fastest way to fix messy indentation.

[= operator](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#shift-left-right)

frontText: Indent / un-indent the current line **while typing in Insert mode**
backText:
```
Ctrl-t    indent one level   (Insert mode)
Ctrl-d    un-indent one level (Insert mode)
```

Works anywhere in the line — no need to move to the start or leave Insert mode.

[Insert mode indenting](https://vimhelp.org/insert.txt.html#i_CTRL-T)

frontText: Configure Vim to indent with **4 spaces instead of tabs**, and convert existing tabs
backText:
```vim
set expandtab      " insert spaces instead of tabs
set shiftwidth=4   " width used by >> << =
set tabstop=4      " how wide a tab displays
set softtabstop=4  " columns the Tab key inserts
:retab             " convert existing tabs in the file
```

Put the `set` lines in `~/.vimrc`; run `:retab` once per file to normalize old content.

['tabstop' and friends](https://vimhelp.org/options.txt.html#%27tabstop%27)

frontText: Paste from / copy to the **system clipboard**
backText:
```
"+p    paste from system clipboard
"+y    yank selection to system clipboard
```

The `+` register is the OS clipboard (requires clipboard support: any Neovim, or Vim with `+clipboard`). Pasting via `"+p` never triggers the auto-indent cascade that terminal paste causes.

[Registers](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#registers)

frontText: Paste code from the terminal **without breaking the indentation** (classic Vim without clipboard support)
backText:
```vim
:set paste     " disable auto-indent/mappings
(paste with the terminal shortcut)
:set nopaste   " re-enable
```

Without this, each pasted line gets re-indented on top of its own leading whitespace, producing the familiar "staircase". Modern Vim/Neovim with bracketed paste usually handles this automatically.

['paste' option](https://vimhelp.org/options.txt.html#%27paste%27)

frontText: Paste a yanked block so that it **matches the indentation** of the current line
backText:
```
]p    paste and adjust indent to current line
```

Alternatively paste normally and re-indent what was just pasted: `` `[v`]= `` (select last pasted text, then auto-indent it).

[]p](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%5Dp)

frontText: Make `y`, `d` and `p` use the **system clipboard automatically**
backText:
```vim
set clipboard=unnamedplus
```

With this in `~/.vimrc` every yank/delete lands in the OS clipboard and `p` pastes from it — no `"+` prefix needed.

['clipboard' option](https://vimhelp.org/options.txt.html#%27clipboard%27)

frontText: Jump between a **parenthesis / bracket / brace and its match**
backText:
```
%    jump to the matching ( ) [ ] { }
```

The cursor may be on the bracket or anywhere before it on the line. Run `:packadd! matchit` (ships with Vim) to make `%` also match HTML tags and `if/endif`-style keywords.

[% motion](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#various-motions)

frontText: Delete / change / copy everything **inside** the parentheses the cursor is in
backText:
```
di(   delete inside ( )
ci(   change inside ( )
yi(   yank inside ( )
da(   delete including the parens
```

Same pattern for other pairs: `i{` `i[` `i"` `i'` `it` (HTML tag). `i` = inner, `a` = around. Works from anywhere inside the pair.

[Text objects](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#text-objects)

frontText: Visually select the **body of the current code block** (inside `{ }`)
backText:
```
vi{   select inside the braces
va{   select including the braces
```

Repeating the text object expands outward: `vi{` then `i{` again selects the enclosing block.

[Text objects](https://vimhelp.org/motion.txt.html#text-objects)

frontText: Jump to the **beginning / end of the current code block** (the unmatched `{` above / `}` below)
**Search** for a pattern forward / backward and step through the matches
backText:
```
/pattern    search forward
?pattern    search backward
n           next match
N           previous match
:noh        clear the highlighting
```

Set `set ignorecase smartcase` to make searches case-insensitive unless the pattern contains a capital.

[Search commands](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#search-commands)

frontText: Replace **all occurrences** of `old` with `new` in the whole file — and with **confirmation** for each
backText:
```vim
:%s/old/new/g     replace all
:%s/old/new/gc    ask before each (y/n/a/q)
```

`%` = whole file, `g` = all matches per line, `c` = confirm. During `c`: `y` yes, `n` no, `a` all remaining, `q` quit.

[:substitute](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3Asubstitute)

frontText: Replace only **within lines 5–20**, and only **within the visual selection**
backText:
```vim
:5,20s/old/new/g      lines 5-20 only
:'<,'>s/old/new/g     visual selection only
```

Select with `V` first — pressing `:` then automatically inserts the `'<,'>` range for you.

[:substitute ranges](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3Asubstitute)

frontText: Replace only **whole-word** matches of `old` (don't touch `older` or `bold`)
backText:
```vim
:%s/\<old\>/new/g
```

`\<` and `\>` anchor the pattern to word boundaries — the same trick `*` uses when searching the word under the cursor.

[Pattern atoms](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#%2F%5C%3C)

frontText: Search for a pattern **across the whole project** and browse the matches
backText:
```vim
:grep -r 'old' src/       " external grep
:vimgrep /old/ **/*.js    " built-in grep
:copen                    " open match list (quickfix)
:cnext  /  :cprev         " jump between matches
```

Both fill the quickfix list. Tip: `set grepprg=rg\ --vimgrep` makes `:grep` use ripgrep.

[Quickfix](https://vimhelp.org/quickfix.txt.html)

frontText: **Find and replace across every file in the project**
backText:
```vim
:grep -r 'old' src/
:cfdo %s/old/new/g | update
```

`:cfdo` runs the substitution in **every file** that has a quickfix match; `| update` saves each changed file. Review the hits with `:copen` before pulling the trigger.

[:cfdo](https://vimhelp.org/quickfix.txt.html#%3Acfdo)

frontText: Rename a variable in the **whole file** without retyping its (possibly long) name
backText:
```
*                 search word under cursor
:%s//newName/g    substitute the last search
```

An **empty pattern** in `:s//` reuses the last search — and `*` already added whole-word anchors, so `count` won't match `counter`.

[:substitute](https://vimhelp.org/change.txt.html#%3Asubstitute)

frontText: Rename a variable **only inside the current function** (stock Vim, no LSP)
backText:
```
gd     go to the variable's local definition
[{     jump to the enclosing block start
V%     select the whole block
:s/\<old\>/new/g
```

`gd` finds the declaration in the current scope; selecting the function body first confines the substitute to that range.

[gd](https://vimhelp.org/pattern.txt.html#gd)

frontText: **Toggle between the two files** you're editing most
backText:
```
Ctrl-^    (Ctrl-6) switch to the alternate file
```

The "alternate" file is the previously edited buffer — pressing it repeatedly bounces between two files, perfect for code + test.

[Alternate file](https://vimhelp.org/editing.txt.html#CTRL-%5E)

frontText: List the open buffers and **switch to one by partial name**
backText:
```vim
:ls           " list open buffers
:b part<Tab>  " switch by partial name, Tab cycles
:bn  /  :bp   " next / previous buffer
:bd           " close (delete) current buffer
```

`:b` matches any substring of the buffer name — `:b ctrl` can jump straight to `user_controller.js`.

[Buffer list](https://vimhelp.org/windows.txt.html#buffer-list)

frontText: Set up **fuzzy file finding in stock Vim** (no plugins) and open a deeply nested file by a few characters of its name
backText:
```vim
" in ~/.vimrc
set path+=**           " :find searches recursively
set wildmenu           " visual completion menu
set wildoptions=fuzzy  " fuzzy matching (Vim 9 / Neovim)

" then:
:find usrctl<Tab>      " matches src/user_controller.js
```

`:find` looks through every directory in `path`; with `**` that's the whole project tree, and fuzzy wildmenu matching makes it behave like a lightweight fuzzy finder.

[:find](https://vimhelp.org/editing.txt.html#%3Afind)

frontText: Open a file by path, with completion
backText:
```vim
:e path/to/file<Tab>
```

`:e` (`:edit`) opens a file; `Tab` completes directories and names (with `wildmenu` you get a visual menu). `:e!` reloads the current file, discarding changes.

[:edit](https://vimhelp.org/editing.txt.html#%3Aedit)
